g.data-narration: Welcome to the Ethereum Stack! This animated lecture will guide you through Ethereum's complete technology stack, from consensus to applications.
The Ethereum Stack is a layered architecture enabling decentralized applications. Understanding each layer is essential for blockchain developers.
g.text: Ethereum is the leading programmable blockchain for dApps, DeFi, and NFTs.
Mastering this stack is essential for building the next generation of web technologies.

g.data-narration: The Ethereum stack has four main layers: Consensus, Execution (EVM), Smart Contracts, and Applications. Each layer builds on the one below it.
Layer 0 is the foundation: Proof of Stake consensus with validators staking 32 ETH, Beacon Chain coordination, and P2P networking for blockchain security.
The Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) is a Turing-complete, stack-based virtual machine that executes smart contract bytecode with gas metering.
The EVM has four main components: Stack (256-bit words), Memory (volatile), Storage (persistent key-value), and Program Counter.
The EVM executes transactions through seven steps: receive, load bytecode, initialize, execute instructions, update state, emit logs, and return result.
g.text: World State: All Account Balances, Storage, and Contract Data

g.data-narration: EVM opcodes include ADD, MUL, PUSH, POP, MSTORE, SSTORE, and CALL. Gas costs vary: ADD is 3 gas, SSTORE is 20,000 gas for new storage.
Smart contracts are self-executing programs on the blockchain with properties: immutable, transparent, trustless, and composable. They auto-enforce agreements.
g.text: Self-executing programs stored on blockchain that automatically enforce agreements

g.data-narration: Solidity is the most popular smart contract language with JavaScript-like syntax. Vyper offers Python-like simplicity for enhanced security.
g.text: pragma solidity ^0.8.0; contract Storage { uint256 data; function set(uint256 x) public { data = x; } }

g.data-narration: ERC standards define token interfaces: ERC-20 for fungible tokens like USDT, ERC-721 for NFTs like CryptoPunks, ERC-1155 for multi-token standards.
Smart contract compilation converts Solidity source code into EVM bytecode and ABI JSON. The bytecode deploys to blockchain; ABI enables frontend interaction.
Layer 3 includes development frameworks (Hardhat, Truffle, Foundry), Web3 libraries (Web3.js, Ethers.js), and node providers (Infura, Alchemy).
Ethers.js enables JavaScript interaction with Ethereum. Key operations include connecting providers, reading balances, and calling contract methods.
Layer 4 is the application layer with wallets (MetaMask), dApps (DeFi, NFTs, DAOs), and frontend interfaces (React, Vue) for end-user interaction.
A dApp architecture flows from frontend (React) through Web3 library to smart contracts, then to the EVM and blockchain consensus layer for execution.
Layer 2 scaling solutions improve Ethereum's throughput: Optimistic Rollups, ZK-Rollups, and sidechains like Polygon reduce fees while maintaining security.
